# Meeting Summary for AI WG

- Meeting start: 2026-06-25T17:23:48Z
- Meeting end: 2026-06-25T18:41:02Z
- Summary start: 2026-06-25T17:23:49Z
- Summary end: 2026-06-25T18:38:34Z

The AI Working Group meeting focused on two main presentations: Pascal's semantic search benchmarking suite and Samuel's GraphQL mock response CLI tool. Pascal presented a comprehensive benchmarking framework for semantic search in GraphQL schemas, which includes 800 test cases across multiple schemas and evaluates different embedding models, templates, and algorithms. The benchmark demonstrated that the "slicer" algorithm could generate optimal sub-schemas in 90% of cases using an average of 10.7K tokens, and GPT-4.1 Mini achieved 85% success in agent-based testing. Samuel then shared his open-sourced MockQL CLI tool, which allows developers to mock GraphQL responses using LLMs and works with both server-side implementations and direct LLM API calls. The group discussed potential collaboration opportunities between the two tools, with plans to continue discussions in the GraphQL Discord community and potentially schedule a follow-up meeting to align on standards and specifications.

## Next Steps

- Samuel: Set up Discord account and join the GraphQL Discord AI-WG channel for further collaboration and discussion on mock tools/specs.
- Samuel & Michael (and interested parties): Continue discussion/collaboration on the @mock directive, mock CLI, and related tools in Discord, and consider scheduling an ad hoc working group meeting to dive deeper into technical collaboration and specification alignment.
- Samuel: Share documentation and commands for the MockQL CLI with the group (as referenced in the meeting).
- Pascal: Bookmark and review Dale's playground/semantic search comparison tool for potential collaboration and benchmarking.
- Dale: Collaborate with Pascal (and others) to integrate/test Dale's embedding/semantic search strategies with the existing benchmark suite.
- Samuel, Mark, Michael, and interested parties: Prepare for a follow-up discussion (at next working group or ad hoc meeting) comparing the different mock solutions, focusing on technical distinctions, constraints, and potential for shared specification/framework plugins (e.g., Apollo Link, Relay plugin).
- Samuel: Save and optionally share the Zoom chat for reference before it is lost after the meeting.
- Mark: Email Samuel connection details for Discord and/or further coordination.
- All interested parties: Consider contributing to or reviewing the mock directive/spec and related CLI implementations, with an eye toward ecosystem-wide collaboration and avoiding redundant effort.

## Summary

### Team Introduction and CLA Compliance

The meeting began with introductions from participants including Dale from Apollo, Samuel from Expedia Group, Michael from Airbnb, Jeff from Apollo, Thore from Germany, Raymie from Airbnb, and Jedko from Dublin. Pascal took over hosting duties from an absent Quay and discussed CLA compliance issues with Raymie's pull request. The meeting was transitioning to agenda items, with Pascal planning to present on semantic search and benchmarks, though the full presentation was not captured in this transcript segment.

### GraphQL Semantic Search Benchmarking

Pascal presented a CLI tool and benchmark suite for evaluating semantic search and retrieval in GraphQL, which includes 800 test cases across multiple schemas like GitHub, GitLab, and Linear. The tool benchmarks different embedding models and templates, with promising results showing that a smaller fine-tuned model (1K parameters) outperformed OpenAI's small and large models. The semantic search algorithm called "slicer" successfully processed 816 test cases with an average of 10.7K tokens, and the agent benchmark demonstrated 85% success rate using GPT-4.1 Mini to answer user questions without additional interaction.

### Semantic Search Benchmarking Tools Discussion

The meeting focused on semantic search benchmarking and GraphQL mock response tools. Pascal presented a benchmark suite for semantic search that can compare different algorithms and models, with Thore expressing interest in analyzing the data differently to gain more insights. Dale shared a playground tool for comparing vector embedding models and BM25 indexing strategies, which Pascal noted was designed to be extensible for algorithm comparisons. Samuel then demonstrated a new CLI tool for generating GraphQL mock responses using LLMs, which he open-sourced as a Rust-based alternative to server implementations, highlighting its flexibility and performance benefits. The group agreed to continue discussions about the mock directive specifications and potential collaboration between the different approaches in an upcoming working group meeting.
